1) INTRODUCTION 
a) Product Name  
 
OMI_CIRCULATION_BOUNDARY_ATLANTIC_gulf_stream_destabilization_point 
 
b) Indicator Evaluation Method 
The identification of the Gulf Stream path is based on  daily Absolute Dynamic 
Topography (ADT) maps from satellite altimetry averaged to produce monthly maps 
with a spatial resolution of 1/8°. Here, the Gulf Stream path was identified with the 25 
cm SSH contour from detrended ADT  according to. e.g., Lillibridge and Mariano 
(2013), Rossby et al. (2014), Andres (2016). Chi et al. (2021), and Guo et al. (2023).  
The yearly Gulf Stream destabilization point is computed following the methodology of 
Andres (2016). Monthly mean Gulf Stream paths are divided into 0.5° longitude bins, 
and the yearly latitude variance of the Gulf Stream’s position is calculated in each 
bin, then the destabilization point is defined as the first downstream longitude where 
the latitude variance reaches 0.42 (°)², equal to half the maximum variance observed 
in the aggregate 1993–2022 data set. This threshold marks the transition from a stable, 
detached jet to an unstable, meandering jet. 
 
For each of the 12 monthly mean paths each year, the destabilization point is 
identified, producing a distribution of 12 values. From this monthly distribution, the 95% 
confidence interval of the annual destabilization point is derived. To minimize spurious 
signals due to high-frequency variability, a 5-year running mean is applied to the 
yearly destabilization time series for both longitude and latitude. 
 
c) Copernicus Marine products used as input for the computation

catalog_entry.summary: This Quality Information Document evaluates the Gulf Stream destabilization point using daily Absolute Dynamic Topography maps from satellite altimetry and monthly mean paths divided into longitude bins to calculate yearly latitude variance.
